stories.headline: Avalanche reacquire Stanley Cup champion Nazem Kadri from Calgary Flames before trade deadline
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche finalized a trade with the Calgary Flames on Friday to bring back center Nazem Kadri, who helped the team win the Stanley Cup in 2022. The Flames will retain 20% of Kadri's contract, giving him a $5.6 million cap hit in Colorado. The Avalanche also receive a 2027 fourth-round pick in the deal.

In return, Colorado sends a conditional 2028 first-round pick (top-10 protected), a conditional 2027 second-round pick, winger Victor Olofsson, and prospect Max Curran to Calgary. The first-round pick protection means the Avalanche would still hold their own first-round selection if they win the draft lottery.

Kadri, 35, has 12 goals and 41 points in 61 games this season with Calgary. His return gives Colorado elite depth down the middle alongside Nathan MacKinnon and Brock Nelson, who already has 30 goals on the year.

The Kadri deal caps a busy trade deadline week for the Avalanche, who also added center Nicolas Roy, defenseman Nick Blankenburg and defenseman Brett Kulak. The moves signal an all-in push for a second Stanley Cup championship.

stories.headline: Winter storm dumps 7.5 inches at DIA, snarls Denver metro commute and causes over 1,000 flight delays
stories.summary:
A significant winter storm moved through the Denver metro area Friday, depositing 7.5 inches of snow at Denver International Airport by evening and creating hazardous driving conditions across the Front Range. The National Weather Service in Boulder issued a Winter Weather Advisory through 8 p.m. for Denver, with 3 to 5 inches forecast for the city and 4 to 6 inches possible in western and southern portions of the metro.

More than 1,011 flights were delayed and 41 canceled at DIA as of Friday afternoon, according to FlightAware. A United Airlines flight was also forced to deplane passengers after the aircraft struck a deicing truck on the ground Friday morning.

Road conditions deteriorated through the day. Northbound I-25 was closed at the Wyoming border due to blowing snow, Highway 285 between Fairplay and Grant was shut down after multiple crashes involving stuck semis, and lanes on I-25 in the south metro were closed following an accident reported by South Metro Fire Rescue.

The storm brought the first meaningful snowfall of a notably warm and dry winter to the Denver metro. Temperatures hovered near freezing throughout the day, with a high of 33 degrees — roughly 19 degrees below the average for March 6.

stories.headline: Diesel contamination of 1.6 million gallons of Denver-area gasoline caused by valve left open, state investigators find
stories.summary:
Workers at the HF Sinclair fuel distribution terminal in Henderson mistakenly left a valve open on Jan. 7, allowing diesel fuel to mix with regular unleaded gasoline and contaminating more than 1.6 million gallons of fuel, state investigators announced Friday. Of that amount, 365,694 gallons were sold to 49 gas stations across the Front Range, including locations operated by King Soopers, Costco and Murphy Express.

More than 1,000 vehicle owners reported problems — including stalling, sputtering and illuminated check engine lights — after refueling at affected stations. Colorado's Division of Oil and Public Safety fined Sinclair $365,694 for the incident, or $1 per gallon of contaminated fuel sold, working with the Colorado Attorney General's office to craft a penalty that exceeded the standard statutory limit of $500 per violation per day.

Sinclair did not notify state regulators of the contamination and did not provide a list of affected gas stations to investigators until Jan. 13, five days after the incident was discovered. The company has since implemented a two-person verification system and a checklist protocol to prevent similar errors.

State officials said ongoing fuel quality monitoring will be required at Sinclair facilities going forward. The incident, which unfolded over roughly 16 hours before employees noticed irregularities reported by gas station operators, was described by a state official as "not a great Friday morning."

stories.headline: Cherry Hills Village officer shoots car theft suspect inside Englewood hospital after violent attack
stories.summary:
A Cherry Hills Village police officer shot a car theft suspect inside HCA HealthONE Swedish hospital in Englewood on Thursday evening after the suspect violently attacked the officer during a medical examination, police said Friday. The officer had been monitoring the suspect, who had been arrested earlier Thursday on suspicion of car theft and taken to the hospital for medical care.

The attack occurred just after 5:30 p.m. No other patients, staff or visitors were injured in the shooting, according to Cherry Hills Village Police Chief Jason Lyons. The suspect remained at Swedish hospital being treated for injuries.

The officer was placed on paid administrative leave, standard department procedure following an officer-involved shooting. The 18th Judicial District will conduct an independent investigation into the incident.

"Violence in a health care setting is unacceptable, and we are grateful that no additional injuries occurred," Chief Lyons said in a statement. He added that the presence of a monitoring officer during medical care of a suspect is standard practice for situations such as this.

stories.headline: U.S. House committee probes fraud and waste in Colorado's Medicaid program, demands response by March 17
stories.summary:
The U.S. House Committee on Energy and Commerce sent a letter to Gov. Jared Polis and the Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Finance on March 3, demanding answers about potential fraud, waste and abuse in the state's Medicaid program. The committee, led by Chair Rep. Brett Guthrie of Kentucky, cited concerns that Colorado's program may be vulnerable to problems similar to those identified in Minnesota, including over-billing, falsified records, identity theft and phantom claims.

The letter highlighted sharp spending increases in specific Medicaid programs. Non-emergency medical transportation costs surged 436% between 2019 and 2025 — reaching $303 million in 2024, up from $70.5 million in 2022 — partly due to incorrect state guidance that misidentified some patients as needing specialty ambulances. The committee also flagged applied behavioral analysis services for children with autism and genetic laboratory services as high-risk areas.

Colorado's total Medicaid spending has surpassed $14.6 billion, of which $8.6 billion comes from the federal government. That figure has doubled in a decade despite enrollment growing only 7%. The state has since corrected the transportation guidance error, which regulators estimate will save $60.5 million in fiscal year 2026-27.

Gov. Polis, through a spokesperson, said Colorado takes Medicaid oversight seriously and is reviewing the letter. The committee set a March 17 deadline for a formal response from state officials.

stories.headline: Colorado measles outbreak grows to 8 cases; 20 exposure sites identified across Broomfield, Denver and beyond
stories.summary:
Colorado's measles outbreak, centered on two Broomfield schools, has grown to eight confirmed cases in 2026, with health officials identifying 20 possible exposure locations across Broomfield, Denver, Frederick, Lafayette, Louisville and Westminster. The sites include Broomfield High School and Broomfield Heights Middle School, Denver International Airport, multiple restaurants, health care facilities, a grocery store, a church and a bowling alley.

The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment said measles symptoms — including fever, cough, runny nose and red eyes — can take up to three weeks to appear, and people can spread the virus before knowing they are infected. The characteristic rash typically appears about four days after a person becomes contagious.

Health officials urge anyone who may have been exposed to call ahead before seeking medical care to reduce the risk of further spread. Two doses of the MMR vaccine provide about 97% protection; partial protection is possible if vaccination occurs within three days of exposure.

Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, a sharp increase over the prior decade in which most years saw zero to two cases. The current outbreak represents a significant early-year total, with officials emphasizing vaccination as the primary tool for containing further spread.

stories.headline: Denver Public Schools community weighs in on proposed policy creating ICE-free safe zones at all district facilities
stories.summary:
Denver Public Schools held a community forum Thursday where residents, parents and students gave public comment on a proposed board policy that would designate all DPS properties — including school buildings, grounds, bus stops and school-sponsored events — as safe zones from immigration enforcement. The policy would prohibit school employees from cooperating with ICE detainer requests and bar school resource officers from sharing student or family information with federal immigration authorities.

Supporters, including students from Abraham Lincoln High School and North High School, said fear of immigration enforcement has affected attendance and mental health among immigrant students. Advocacy groups such as Movimento Poder said young people are experiencing heightened anxiety about being detained, which is disrupting their education.

Opponents at the meeting argued the district should not shield individuals who have broken immigration laws from enforcement and said the policy itself creates fear and undermines respect for law enforcement.

DPS is Colorado's largest school district. The proposal comes as immigration enforcement operations have intensified nationally, and as Denver City Council recently passed a separate ordinance banning masks for ICE agents and other law enforcement operating within city limits. No vote on the DPS policy has been scheduled.

stories.headline: Federal judge dismisses homebuilder's constitutional challenge to Denver affordable housing fee ordinances
stories.summary:
U.S. District Court Judge Philip A. Brimmer on March 3 dismissed a lawsuit brought by homebuilders redT Homes and redT Capital Partners challenging the constitutionality of two Denver ordinances that impose fees and affordable housing requirements on residential developers. The companies had sued the city over fees of approximately $25,000 and $45,000 tied to two proposed six-home developments, arguing the charges amounted to an unconstitutional "taking" of private property.

The plaintiffs contended Denver's linkage fee ordinance — which charges per-square-foot fees on new housing developments with fewer than 10 units — lacked a sufficient connection between the fees and the actual impact of the development, as required under U.S. Supreme Court precedent. Denver argued the fees address the affordable housing demand created by new construction and job growth.

Judge Brimmer agreed that the proportionality of the linkage fees would need further scrutiny, but concluded that the city's mandatory affordable housing ordinance provides a constitutional alternative path to compliance — developers may designate a portion of units as affordable housing rather than pay fees. That alternative rendered the overall ordinance constitutional, he found.

The ruling is a victory for Denver's efforts to fund affordable housing through developer contributions. The city council's ordinance states that housing cost increases in Denver are driven in part by population and job growth that has outpaced affordable housing supply.

stories.headline: Denver police chase stolen vehicle onto I-70 in Aurora, officer's foot run over during stop
stories.summary:
A Denver police pursuit of a stolen vehicle ended on eastbound Interstate 70 in Aurora on Thursday evening after the driver fled a traffic stop near 45th Avenue and Zuni Street and ran over an officer's foot in the process. Officers pursued the vehicle and used a forced stop to end the chase west of Chambers Road.

The driver was arrested and transported to a hospital for evaluation. The officer whose foot was struck was also taken to a hospital. No additional details on injuries were released.

The incident closed eastbound I-70 at Interstate 225 beginning at 4:51 p.m., with one lane reopening by 6:15 p.m. and full reopening by 7:30 p.m., according to the Colorado Department of Transportation. The closure caused significant traffic backups during the evening commute.

stories.headline: State patrol releases preliminary findings in fatal 30-vehicle dust storm pileup near Pueblo on I-25
stories.summary:
Colorado State Patrol on Friday released a preliminary report on the fatal multi-vehicle crash that occurred Feb. 17 on Interstate 25 near Pueblo, involving more than 30 vehicles. Investigators believe the chain reaction began when a Ford Escape rear-ended a slower-moving GMC Sierra hauling a trailer — the Sierra was traveling 15 to 20 mph in a 75-mph zone — under "brown-out" dust storm conditions that severely reduced visibility.

After the initial collision, a Kenworth semi-truck slowed for the crash and was sideswiped by a Ram 3500 hauling a stock trailer, which became detached. The Ram continued onto the shoulder and struck the original Sierra. The blockage created by those vehicles then triggered a series of additional collisions involving numerous other vehicles.

State patrol confirmed the crash resulted in fatalities and injuries but did not specify the final counts in Friday's update, stating that the timeline for some involved vehicles remains under investigation. The full investigation is ongoing.

stories.headline: Nuggets survive Lakers' fourth-quarter rally, win 120-113 behind Jokic triple-double and Murray's 28 points
stories.summary:
Nikola Jokic delivered a clutch 8-footer with 22.3 seconds remaining Thursday to seal the Denver Nuggets' 120-113 victory over the Los Angeles Lakers, keeping the Nuggets 1.5 games ahead of Los Angeles for fifth place in the Western Conference. Jokic finished with 28 points, 12 rebounds and 13 assists, though he also committed nine turnovers.

Jamal Murray matched Jokic's 28 points, and Julian Strawther added 18 in his second consecutive start filling in for injured rotation players. The Nuggets played without four regular contributors — Peyton Watson, Aaron Gordon, Spencer Jones and Cam Johnson — forcing coach David Adelman to use an unconventional lineup including Zeke Nnaji as a starter.

Luka Doncic scored 27 points on 24 shots for Los Angeles but was held in check during critical late possessions. LeBron James briefly exited with an injury in the fourth quarter; during the first frame he surpassed Kareem Abdul-Jabbar for the most field goals made in NBA history.

The win was Denver's second in a row and came on the same night the Nuggets debuted newly signed point guard Tyus Jones, who played six minutes and recorded one assist. The Nuggets improve to 39-24 on the season.

stories.headline: Colorado bill would require agencies to better track and address barriers facing older job seekers
stories.summary:
A bill advancing through the Colorado legislature would require the Colorado Commission on Aging and the Colorado Workforce Development Council to meet more frequently to discuss employment barriers for workers 55 and older, identify trends and propose solutions to lawmakers. House Bill 26-1010, sponsored by state Rep. Jenny Willford, D-34, would also mandate that certain advisory boards include at least one member age 55 or older actively engaged in older-worker issues.

The measure would additionally direct state agencies to improve data collection on age discrimination in hiring — an issue advocates say is widespread but difficult to document because of inconsistent reporting practices. Supporters testified about being passed over for jobs despite strong qualifications, with one 74-year-old actor recounting being told by interviewers they did not think he could "keep up."

Colorado's population over 65 is projected to more than double between 2010 and 2050, according to a Common Sense Institute report, raising concerns about workforce participation rates and growing demand for social services. Willford said the bill reflects the reality that Coloradans are living and working longer and need systems designed to support them.

The bill does not carry a fiscal note and has no formal opposition on record as of Friday. It fits within the state's broader ten-year Multi-Sector Plan on Aging.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: The only marathon I'll ever run is a Netflix marathon. And even then, I'll need a break halfway through.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Nuggets vs. Los Angeles Lakers
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Nuggets host the Los Angeles Lakers at Ball Arena in an NBA regular season matchup.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Museum of Nature & Science Free Evening
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Museum of Nature & Science offers free admission from 4 to 9 p.m. as part of an SCFD Free Day.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Museum of Nature & Science, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Denver Restaurant Week
weekendEvents.description: The 22nd annual Denver Restaurant Week features multi-course meals at more than 300 participating restaurants priced at $25 to $55. The event runs March 6–15, with this weekend covering the opening days.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Golf Expo
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Golf Expo features pro demos, mini golf courses, gear shopping, and interactive games for all ages at Denargo Market.

weekendEvents.title: Mile High Beer Fest
weekendEvents.description: More than 40 local and national breweries including Left Hand Brewing Co., Renegade Brewing, and Eddyline Brewery offer unlimited samples at Denargo Market.

weekendEvents.title: Blarney on Belmar
weekendEvents.description: A free, family-friendly St. Patrick's Day festival featuring live music from The Elders and Tadhg & Friends, Irish step dancers, a Lucky Clover Market, and festive food and drink from local breweries.

weekendEvents.title: SeriesFest Fashion in Focus with Molly Rogers
weekendEvents.description: Emmy-winning costume designer Molly Rogers, known for 'Sex and the City,' 'And Just Like That…,' and 'The Devil Wears Prada,' receives the Excellence in Costume Design Award from SeriesFest alongside a runway show of iconic TV and film costumes. Tickets starting at $125 include a conversation with Rogers, cocktail party, and full fashion show.
weekendEvents.location: The Cable Center, University of Denver, 2000 Buchtel Blvd., Denver

weekendEvents.title: Denver Brass: Celtic Mania
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Brass presents its Celtic Mania program featuring thunderous brass, blazing bagpipes, and electrifying dance performances from Celtic Colorado Pipes & Drums, Rocky Mountain Highland Dancers, and Celtic Steps Irish Dance School. Two performances Saturday, one Sunday.
weekendEvents.location: Newman Center for the Performing Arts, Denver

weekendEvents.title: No Man's Land Film Festival
weekendEvents.description: The premier all-woman and gender-nonconforming adventure film festival celebrates athletes and adventurers by expanding the definition of femininity in adventure, sport, and conservation.

weekendEvents.title: Leadville Ski Joring
weekendEvents.description: Skiers are pulled through a course by riders on horseback, hitting jumps and collecting rings in this high-energy Colorado tradition. Free to attend; no dogs allowed.

weekendEvents.title: Demetri Martin: Quick Draw Tour
weekendEvents.description: Comedian and actor Demetri Martin headlines the Paramount Theatre on his Quick Draw Tour, featuring his signature blend of one-liners, wordplay, and on-stage drawings.

weekendEvents.title: Molly Brown House Museum Free Admission
weekendEvents.description: The Molly Brown House Museum offers free admission on International Women's Day as part of an SCFD Free Day, allowing visitors to tour the historic home of one of Colorado's most prominent women.

topStory.headline: Trump Demands Iran's 'Unconditional Surrender' as U.S. War Enters Second Week
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump declared Friday that the United States will accept nothing less than Iran's "unconditional surrender," rejecting the prospect of a negotiated settlement as the week-old air war known as Operation Epic Fury intensified. Trump posted the demand on his Truth Social platform, adding that following any surrender the U.S. and its allies would work to install new Iranian leadership and rebuild the country economically. The statement came the same day Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian confirmed that third-party nations are engaged in mediation efforts, though he stressed Iran is "committed to peace" while remaining prepared to defend itself.

U.S. Central Command reported that American forces have struck more than 3,000 targets since the operation began last Saturday, damaging or destroying 43 Iranian ships in the process. Six U.S. service members have been killed in the conflict, and Trump is expected to attend their dignified transfer at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware on Saturday. The deadliest single incident of the war remains a strike on a girls' primary school in Minab on the opening day of fighting, which Iranian authorities say killed approximately 180 pupils and staff; U.S. investigators now believe American forces may have been responsible because the U.S. was operating in the area while Israel was not, though the White House said the inquiry is ongoing and no final conclusions have been reached.

The conflict has produced significant geopolitical fallout beyond the immediate battlefield. Four sources with knowledge of the matter told NBC News that Russia is providing Iran with satellite and other intelligence data on the location of U.S. forces in the region, a development that could help Tehran target American warships and bases, though there is no indication Moscow is directly guiding Iranian strikes. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the administration is "tracking everything" and that the commander-in-chief is "well aware of who's talking to who," signaling that the intelligence-sharing arrangement has been factored into U.S. battle planning.

The war has sent global oil prices sharply higher, prompting the Treasury Department to grant India a 30-day license to purchase some sanctioned Russian oil. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent indicated Friday that additional Russian oil sanctions could be lifted to ease supply pressures. A dozen Senate Democrats, including Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, criticized that move, arguing it gives Russian President Vladimir Putin "windfall profits" and accused Trump of creating a "self-made global energy shock" through what they called reckless military action.

Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz and has been launching missiles and drones at U.S. bases and Israeli and Gulf targets throughout the week, according to Al Jazeera. UNICEF reported at least 1,332 people killed in Iran, including 181 children. The U.S. Embassy in Baghdad issued a security alert Friday urging American citizens to leave Iraq, warning that Iran-aligned militant groups may seek to attack Americans and that commercial flights out of the country have been suspended, with overland routes to Jordan, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and Turkey remaining the primary options for departure.
